turns.text: Hello Evangeline, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're going through the birth experience, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

It’s a bit surreal to be on this side of the desk, honestly. Usually, I’m the one handing out the pamphlets, talking parents through the "what-ifs," and maintaining that calm, professional veneer. Now, the nursery is ready, the bags are sitting by the door, and I’m finding it impossible to turn off my brain. 

People look at my chart—low risk, healthy, textbook—and they see a "perfect" patient. They assume I’m not anxious because I’ve seen this a thousand times. But the reality is, knowing the mechanics of birth doesn’t make the surrender any easier. When I’m at my check-ups, I have to fight the urge to glance at the monitor to check the fetal heart rate myself, or to dissect the midwife’s choice of language. I’m constantly teetering between being a participant and an observer. 

I worry that I’ve spent so much time being the expert that I’ve forgotten how to just *be* the mother. My biggest fear isn't the pain—I know the physiology of that inside and out. It’s the fear that once the adrenaline of birth wears off, I’ll be so exhausted that I’ll lose access to all the knowledge I’ve spent my career accumulating. I’m ready to start, though. I just want to stop analyzing and start feeling. Let’s talk.

Before labour, what did you expect creating and using a birth plan to be like — how it would be received by staff and how much it would guide your care? And what actually happened with your birth plan during labour?

I approached my birth plan with the kind of meticulous, slightly obsessive detail you’d expect from a pediatric nurse. I typed it up, color-coded it, and even left space for "preferences" rather than "demands" because I know how frustrating it is for staff when a patient insists on a rigid protocol that ignores clinical reality. I fully expected it to act as a bridge—a way to say, "I know the risks, but I’d like to try this path first." I assumed the staff would appreciate that I knew the terminology, that I wouldn't need things explained in layperson’s terms.

But when I was actually in the thick of it, the plan felt like a scrap of paper that had nothing to do with the visceral reality of my body. The moment the contractions started hitting with real intensity, my "professional" brain—the part that wanted to track and manage everything—just shattered. I found myself wanting someone else to take the lead, to tell me what to do, even though I knew the protocols as well as they did. I didn't want to be the "expert" anymore; I just wanted to be cared for. The plan became irrelevant because the experience became about survival and surrender, not about my preferences for lighting or delayed cord clamping. It was humbling.

Before labour, what did you expect your pain management options to be — the range available, how they'd be offered, and how you'd be supported in deciding? And what was the reality of pain management during your labour?

Before I went into labor, I felt like I had a menu of options stored in my head, categorized by pharmacokinetics and contraindications. I expected a collaborative discussion where I’d weigh the pros and cons—you know, the usual professional dialogue. I thought I’d be able to "time" my request for an epidural with the precision of a shift change, holding out for as long as possible to keep my mobility, then pivoting if things got too intense. I genuinely believed I could be the one to orchestrate the timing, because I’ve watched so many women go through it.

The reality, though, was that pain has a way of erasing your credentials. When I was in the middle of active labor, I wasn't a nurse; I was just a person in a storm. My resolve to hold off on intervention just evaporated the moment my body took over. The "range of options" I had studied felt theoretical and distant. I didn't want a clinical consultation; I wanted someone to just *know* I was at my limit. There was a moment where I felt a strange sense of shame—like I had "failed" to handle it the way I’d advised so many others to—but then that faded into a desperate need for relief. It wasn't about the *choice* anymore; it was about the necessity of surviving the next wave.
Were there pain management options you wanted but weren't able to access? What got in the way?

That’s a complicated question, because in my head, I knew exactly what was available. I could have technically asked for anything on the menu—nitrous, IV analgesia, the epidural. It wasn't that the hospital *denied* me access; it was that my own expectations stood in the way.

I had this internal script that said, "You are a nurse; you know how to manage discomfort. Don't be the patient who folds early." I felt a ridiculous amount of pressure to embody a sort of stoic professionalism. I wanted to try these more natural, movement-based approaches—using the birth ball, the tub—but when the pain peaked, I found myself paralyzed. I didn't want to ask for the epidural early because I feared it would be seen as a "medical failure" on my part, or worse, that the staff would look at me and think, *Look at the nurse who couldn't even handle her own birth.*

Looking back, the barrier wasn't the institution; it was my own ego. I was so busy trying to maintain my identity as the expert that I wasn't advocating for my own comfort. I waited longer than I probably should have because I was trying to "perform" a birth that aligned with my professional ideals. It’s an exhausting way to suffer.

When you arrived at the birth setting — whether hospital, birth centre, or home — what did you expect it to feel like in terms of safety, comfort, and how you'd be welcomed? And what was your actual experience of the environment when you arrived?

I’ve spent years walking those hospital corridors, so I walked in with a very specific, clinical map in my head. I expected to feel "safe" because I knew exactly where the crash cart was, how the monitors were calibrated, and what the night shift rhythm looked like. I pictured myself being greeted as a colleague, someone who could step in and out of the "patient" role, nodding at the familiar routines.

The reality, however, was a jarring shift in perspective. When I walked through those doors, the hospital didn’t feel like a workplace anymore; it felt like an alien landscape. Everything looked the same, yet it all felt terrifyingly different. The safety I usually associated with the equipment felt like a double-edged sword—those monitors weren't just data points anymore; they were watching my baby. 

I wasn't welcomed as a peer; I was welcomed as a woman in pain, and that transition was actually quite difficult. Being a nurse, I’m used to being the one in control, the one providing comfort. When I realized that my professional status meant absolutely nothing to the contractions, I felt a deep, hollow sense of vulnerability. It was quieter and colder than I expected, and for the first time, the clinical efficiency I usually admired felt isolating. I didn't want to be a nurse in a gown; I just wanted to be held.

If your birth involved an unexpected intervention or complication, what did you expect the process of being informed and involved in those decisions to look like? And how were those decisions actually made and communicated in the moment?

Even as a nurse, you have this naive belief that when an intervention is needed, there will be a nice, calm sit-down chat. I expected a mini-conference: "Evangeline, we’re seeing 'X' on the monitor, so we’re suggesting 'Y'—what do you think?" I wanted that illusion of partnership, where my input was treated as a valid clinical variable.

But when things started to veer off the path—a sudden deceleration in the heart rate that wouldn't recover—the "process" was not a dialogue. It was a frantic, necessary pivot to urgency. The room changed in a heartbeat. The soft, expectant atmosphere was replaced by a sharp, directive energy. There was no time for the nuanced debate I had fantasized about. The midwife didn't need a nurse's perspective; she needed a mother to trust her immediately so she could act.

I was told, not asked, and in that moment, I realized my professional knowledge was actually a trap. I knew exactly what that drop in heart rate meant, and that terrifying clinical clarity made the lack of "involvement" feel even more acute. I knew the protocol perfectly, so I saw every move they made before they even verbalized it. It was a strange, disassociated feeling: watching my own care unfold with the detached precision of a seasoned clinician, while the mother in me just felt small and completely out of control.
Did you feel you had genuine choice and sufficient information in those moments, or did decisions feel like they happened to you rather than with you?

It’s hard to reconcile those two things. If I look at it through my nurse’s lens, I know the protocols, and I know why they moved the way they did. Everything they did was medically sound. But if I look at it as the mother in that bed? No, it didn't feel like a choice. It felt like I was being carried along by a current that was much faster and more powerful than I was.

When things got critical, there wasn't room for "sufficient information" to be shared in the way a person typically needs to feel empowered. It wasn't a conversation; it was a series of commands designed to save time. I understood the language they were using—that’s the curse of my background—but that didn't make the experience any less disempowering. Because I already knew what was coming, the information didn't help me process it; it just fueled my fear. I saw the signs, I knew the "what," and I knew the "why," but I had absolutely no agency over the "how."

The decisions were made *for* me. In the moment, it felt like being a passenger in a car where the driver is doing exactly the right thing to avoid a crash, but you have no hand on the wheel and no voice in the navigation. I wasn't an active participant in those final, urgent choices; I was the landscape they were navigating.

During labour, what did you expect the midwife or doctor present to provide in terms of emotional support and personal attention — not just clinical care? And what did you actually experience from the staff who were with you?

I think I went in with a bit of a biased expectation. I’ve seen how busy the units get, so I told myself I didn’t want to be "that" patient—the one who needed constant reassurance. I expected them to be professional, efficient, and to offer that kind of clinical warmth where they smile and explain things, then move to the next patient. I thought I’d be fine with just the standard level of care, because I’ve been the one delivering that same level of care to others.

The reality, however, was that when I was in the thick of it, "clinical warmth" just didn't cut it. I didn't need a professional; I needed a witness. When the pain became all-consuming, I realized I was starving for something deeper—I wanted someone to actually *see* my fear, not just monitor my vitals. 

Most of the staff were wonderful in a clinical sense, but there was a distance there. They saw "Evangeline the Nurse," so they kept their interactions relatively objective. They assumed I understood, so they skipped the gentle, humanizing reassurances I actually craved. It was a strange kind of loneliness. I was surrounded by people who were technically excellent, but I felt like I had to maintain my "professional" persona just to keep the dynamic comfortable for them. I think they saw the uniform, even when I wasn't wearing one.

Before birth, what did you expect the immediate period after your baby was born to be like — skin-to-skin contact, who would be present, what would be done? And what actually happened in those first moments after delivery?

I had that hour—the "golden hour"—practically scripted in my mind. I envisioned a quiet, dimly lit room where my baby would be placed directly onto my chest, skin-to-skin, while we just breathed each other in. I expected to be fully present, soaking in the smell of his hair, while my husband held my hand and the staff stayed in the background, only intervening if absolutely necessary. I’ve taught the benefits of that immediate bonding a hundred times; I knew the physiology, the oxytocin surge, the regulation of the infant's temperature. I felt entitled to that moment.

But the reality was chaotic. Because of the complications that led to the urgency, the "golden hour" was effectively stolen. The moment he was born, he was whisked away for a quick assessment and some respiratory support. The room, which I had hoped would feel like a sanctuary, was suddenly full of people—neonatal team members, extra nurses, all moving with a precision that was life-saving but profoundly jarring. 

I didn't get to hold him immediately. I remember watching from the bed, my body still shaking from the labor, and feeling this hollow ache. I wasn't just a mother witnessing her child; I was a professional watching a clinical procedure. I was tracking his O2 stats in my head instead of just being his mother. It was agonizing to be physically present but emotionally sidelined. I eventually got the skin-to-skin time I wanted, but the "perfect" picture in my head was replaced by the cold, bright reality of an intensive care environment.
Before giving birth, what did you expect your physical recovery to look and feel like in the weeks after? And how did the reality of your physical recovery compare to those expectations?

I think I fell into the trap of the "idealized recovery" that we often present to patients—the idea that if you’re healthy and motivated, the body just bounces back. I had this timeline in my head: the first week for rest, the second for light movement, and by the fourth week, I’d be settling into a rhythm of gentle activity. I expected to manage the pain with basic analgesics and return to my "expert" self, navigating postpartum life with the same organized, systematic approach I used for everything else. I genuinely thought my clinical background would help me breeze through the recovery, as if my knowledge could protect me from the physical toll.

The reality hit me like a wall. My body felt like a stranger—tender, exhausted, and completely uncooperative. The "weeks of rest" weren't tranquil recovery periods; they were a blur of sensory overload and relentless physical depletion. The most humbling part was how hard it was to even remember the advice I used to give. When you’re nursing around the clock and your stitches are burning, all the "best practices" about sleep hygiene and self-care feel like distant, theoretical concepts. I wasn't an expert anymore; I was just a woman trying to heal while her entire world shifted. It was much slower, much messier, and infinitely more humbling than any textbook ever let on.
Was there a physical aspect of recovery that no one had prepared you for, or that surprised you significantly?

You hear people talk about the "fourth trimester"—the night sweats, the soreness, the fatigue—but honestly? It’s the mental fragmentation that completely blindsided me. I expected the physical pain, but I didn't expect the way my brain would just... fail to synthesize information. 

I’ve spent years explaining to new mothers how to monitor their baby’s output, their weight gain, and their latch. But when I was home, sleep-deprived and physically broken, I would look at a wet diaper and have to pause for a solid thirty seconds just to calculate what it meant. I had the knowledge, but I had no *bandwidth*. That realization—that I was so depleted I couldn't even utilize my own training—was a visceral, physical shock. It wasn't just my body recovering; it was my identity. 

I was also unprepared for how deeply the postpartum anxiety would manifest as physical tension. I’d be holding him, and my shoulders would be up around my ears, my jaw clenched so tight it ached, because I was constantly "assessing" him like a patient. I couldn't turn off the clinician in me, and that constant, low-level hyper-vigilance made my physical recovery feel twice as long. It felt like I was working a twenty-four-hour shift in the NICU every single day, without ever getting to clock out.

Before your baby arrived, what did you expect feeding — breastfeeding or formula feeding — to be like in terms of ease, support, and how you'd feel about it? And what was the reality of feeding in the early weeks?

I honestly thought I had this one "in the bag." I’ve supported dozens of parents through the mechanics of latching, the engorgement, the supply concerns—I knew the jargon, the positions, the biology of lactation by heart. I figured that because I knew the theory, the practice would be a natural extension of my professional life. I expected it to be a quiet, bonding, and yes, slightly clinical process that I could manage with my eyes closed.

The reality was a complete collapse of my confidence. My baby was tired, and I was so physically and emotionally depleted from the birth that I couldn't "perform" the latching techniques I’d taught others a thousand times. Every time he cried, I didn't see a hungry baby; I saw a case study in failure. I was analyzing every suck-swallow-breathe pattern, checking for signs of fatigue, counting minutes, and obsessing over my own supply like it was a shift-change report. 

It was exhausting. I felt like a failure because I *knew* the "right" way to do it, and my body—and my baby—weren't cooperating with the textbook. I ended up needing a lactation consultant, and that was the most humbling, crushing moment. I had to let go of the expert persona and just sit there, crying while a stranger showed me exactly what I’d been showing other women for years. It turns out, reading the book is nothing like living it.
Before your baby arrived, what did you expect your emotional and mental health to be like in the postpartum period? And what was your actual emotional experience in the weeks and months after birth?

I expected to be the "stable" one. I thought I’d be that composed mother who understood the hormones, knew the signs of the "baby blues," and could objectively monitor my own baseline. I told myself that because I worked in pediatrics, I had a sort of immunity, or at least a professional distance, that would keep the anxiety at bay. I anticipated a steep learning curve, sure, but I imagined I’d approach it with a calm, analytical mindset.

The reality was a complete unraveling. It wasn’t just "the blues"; it was a relentless, high-octane anxiety that I couldn't clock out from. My mind turned into a constant, ticking monitor. Every tiny sound he made triggered a fight-or-flight response, not because I was being a good, attentive nurse, but because I was being a terrified, exhausted person. I didn't feel like a caregiver; I felt like a patient under surveillance by my own brain. 

It was deeply isolating because I felt I couldn't admit to any of my colleagues that I was struggling. I was the person they called for advice, the "expert" who had it all handled. Admitting I was drowning felt like a betrayal of my own professional identity. It was a heavy, suffocating weight—a constant, nagging fear that if I didn't act "normal," I wouldn't be seen as a competent mother.
Before becoming a parent, what did you expect your sense of identity to feel like after your baby arrived? And how has your sense of who you are actually shifted since then?

I think I lived in this bubble of certainty. I was "Evangeline, the pediatric nurse," and I honestly believed that identity would be a shield—a sort of armor that would keep me grounded once the baby arrived. I expected motherhood to be an *addition* to my professional life, not a complete overhaul. I thought I’d just shift into the role, seamlessly moving between "nurse" and "mom" like flipping a switch.

The reality? That identity has completely dissolved, and it’s terrifying. I’m no longer the person who knows what to do; I’m the person who is *feeling* what to do, and those are two very different states of existence. The professional armor I relied on—the checklists, the clinical assessments, the objective distance—it all feels like a hollow shell now. 

I’ve had to confront the fact that I actually hate being the "expert" in my own home. I don't want to assess my child’s development markers; I want to just be his mother. But shedding that persona is harder than I thought. I find myself mourning the woman I was before, who felt so in control of her life, even while I’m discovering this raw, messy, and much deeper version of myself. I feel like I’m in a constant state of transition, still trying to figure out which parts of my old self are worth keeping and which parts have to be left in the hospital.
After birth, what did you expect the postnatal check-ups and follow-up care from your midwife or GP to provide? And what did that post-birth care actually look like in practice?

I walked into my six-week check-up expecting a clinical summary—a quick review of my healing progress, a check of my blood pressure, and a nod toward "everything looks normal." I anticipated that familiar, polite exchange where I’d confirm I was doing fine, because that’s what patients usually say, and I’ve been on the receiving end of that "fine" so many times. I thought the care would be as objective as the prenatal visits.

In reality, the follow-up felt startlingly brief and, in some ways, incredibly hollow. It was essentially a physical audit—a look at the scar, a confirmation that I wasn't hemorrhaging, and a check-off on the postpartum screening tools. There was no space for the "whole person" check-in I now realize I desperately needed. 

When I sat there, trying to articulate the crushing weight of the anxiety I was feeling, the conversation felt redirected toward the physical—healing markers and contraception. I felt like a set of vitals being processed rather than a person transitioning into motherhood. It was an exercise in efficiency that left me feeling completely unseen. I wanted someone to ask, "How are *you* holding up?" and really wait for the answer, not just look for the "all clear" on a checklist. It confirmed my biggest fear: once the baby is physically stable, the focus shifts away from the mother entirely.
Were there concerns you raised at postnatal appointments that you felt were not taken seriously or properly followed up?

It’s funny, the language we use—"concerns." When I brought up the racing heart, the intrusive thoughts, and the sheer, bone-deep exhaustion that didn't feel like normal fatigue, I didn't frame them as "concerns." I framed them as clinical data. I used the right terminology, almost as if I were giving a patient report to a colleague, hoping that if I presented it with enough objectivity, it would be taken seriously as a medical issue rather than just "new mom nerves."

But the reaction was always the same soft, dismissive smile. They’d say something like, "That’s just the adjustment period, Evangeline. You know how it is—your body is still recovering." 

They weren't *wrong*, but they weren't *listening*. Because they knew I was a pediatric nurse, they treated me like I was "in the club." They assumed that because I understood the physiology of postpartum recovery, I had the resources to handle the psychological fallout. They saw me as a peer who just needed a bit of reassurance, rather than a patient who was struggling to stay afloat. They didn't dig deeper. They didn't ask the follow-up questions that would have actually cracked the shell I was hiding behind. I felt invisible in my own distress, dismissed by my own peers because I knew too much to be seen as "vulnerable."
If you returned to work after your baby was born, what did you expect that transition to be like — practically and emotionally? And what did returning to work actually feel like?

I had convinced myself that returning to the hospital would be a relief—a return to a space where I was competent, valued, and, most importantly, not solely responsible for keeping a tiny human alive. I expected the transition to feel like stepping back into a well-oiled machine where my clinical instincts would take over, providing a much-needed break from the emotional chaos of being a new mom. I thought I’d feel empowered, like I was reclaiming the part of myself that had been dormant for months.

The reality was a jarring, physical dissociation. When I walked back into the unit, I didn't feel empowered; I felt like I was looking at the patients through a completely different lens. Every time I heard an infant cry, it didn't just register as a clinical sign—it echoed in my own chest. I was hyper-aware of the parents in the rooms, watching their faces, their exhaustion, and their fear, and it felt like I was seeing my own trauma mirrored back at me a hundred times a day.

I felt like an imposter. I was managing care, giving instructions, and navigating shifts, but underneath the scrubs, I felt raw and exposed. Every decision I made at work felt heavier because I was constantly comparing it to my own experience. I wanted my professional armor back, but I realized I had left it behind, and I wasn’t sure how to build it again. It wasn't a return to "normal"; it was a struggle to balance two versions of myself that now felt fundamentally incompatible.
Were there practical aspects of returning to work — feeding, childcare, your own health — that were harder to manage than you expected?

The practical logistics were a nightmare I wasn't prepared for, despite knowing all the "best practices." I had this internal blueprint: pump at specified intervals, maintain a steady supply, balance the shifts. But trying to execute a clinical pumping schedule in a fast-paced unit is a different kind of torture. Finding a private space felt like an intrusion on the workflow, and every time I had to step away, I felt that familiar, heavy guilt—like I was failing my patients *and* my baby.

Then there was the physical toll of the night shifts. I expected the fatigue, but I didn't anticipate how it would manifest in my own recovery. My body felt like it was constantly in a state of alert, never truly shifting out of "caregiver mode." I’d leave my shift, go straight to picking up my son, and try to switch into "mom mode," but my brain felt like it was splitting in two. 

My own health became an afterthought. I was so busy managing everyone else's stability that I forgot to eat, forgot to hydrate, and definitely ignored my own aches. I found myself in the breakroom, staring at a lukewarm coffee, realizing that I was applying the "patient care" model to myself and failing miserably. The disconnect between what I’d spent my career teaching and what I was physically capable of doing was staggering. It wasn't just a scheduling conflict; it was a total collapse of my own self-care infrastructure.

After your baby arrived, what did you expect your relationship with your partner to be like in terms of sharing caregiving, managing the relationship, and supporting each other? And what has that relationship dynamic actually looked like?

I expected us to be a synchronized team. We’d discussed the division of labor—the feedings, the sleep shifts, the household maintenance—as if it were a clinical protocol we could just implement. I truly believed that because we’d communicated so clearly beforehand, we’d navigate the transition with that same level of mutual support. I anticipated that we’d be "in it together," checking in on each other’s mental state at the end of the day, as if it were a debriefing session.

The reality has been far more jagged. We aren't a perfectly calibrated team; we’re two exhausted, terrified people trying to keep our heads above water. The division of labor we planned became a source of constant friction because, in the middle of the night, when the baby is screaming and you haven't slept in four hours, "equity" is impossible to track. 

Instead of checking in, we often pass each other like ships in the night, both of us so depleted that we have nothing left to give the other. I catch myself resenting his ability to "switch off" or his lack of that constant, buzzing physiological anxiety that I can’t seem to turn down. We’ve had to learn how to be a couple again in the wreckage of our old routines, and that’s been harder than any clinical shift I’ve ever worked. It’s not about being efficient anymore; it’s about being vulnerable, and that’s a skill we’re both still struggling to learn.
What was the biggest surprise about how your partnership functioned after the baby arrived?

The biggest, most painful surprise was realizing that my medical knowledge actually created a barrier between us. Before the baby, I thought my expertise would be an asset—that I could translate the baby’s cries or interpret his cues for my husband, making us a more efficient team. Instead, it made me the "authority" and him the "assistant." I would reflexively correct his swaddle or critique the way he paced the room, falling into that nurse-preceptor role without even realizing I was doing it.

I didn't expect that I would inadvertently strip him of his confidence by being the "expert." It wasn't until he finally snapped one night—telling me he felt like he was just helping *me* with *my* project—that I realized what I was doing. I was managing him like a junior nurse on the unit instead of partnering with him as a father. 

It was a devastating realization. I had been so busy trying to manage the environment and ensure "clinical success" that I had pushed my husband into a corner of inadequacy. We had to work incredibly hard to deconstruct that dynamic. I had to learn how to be "just" a mother and let him be a father in his own way, even if his way didn't follow the textbook. It’s been a long process of unlearning that professional control to actually make room for our partnership.

Looking across your entire maternity journey — from trying to conceive through to where you are now — what did you expect the overall experience of maternity care to deliver? And reflecting on the whole journey, what was the most significant gap between what you expected and what you received?

I expected the entire journey to be a well-choreographed transition—a bridge between my professional life and my personal life, where my knowledge acted as a safety net. I thought I was entering a space that would validate my expertise, acknowledge my clinical perspective, and ultimately guide me into motherhood with grace and clinical precision. I expected the care to be a partnership of equals, where my understanding of the "system" would translate into a more empowered, controlled experience.

The most significant gap, looking back, is that I was treated as a "colleague" when I needed to be treated as a "mother." That is the fundamental heartbreak. Because I spoke their language, the staff gave me clinical information, but they failed to offer me the human care—the emotional containment and validation—that any woman in that vulnerability deserves. They saw the nurse, not the person in pain. 

I feel like I spent the entire journey in a sterile, professional limbo. I didn't get the "expert" experience I thought I’d be able to manage, and I didn't get the compassionate, nurturing support I didn't know I needed until it was too late. I was an insider in the system, but that’s exactly what made me the biggest outsider of all. I just wanted to be held, not consulted.
If you could change one thing about how the maternity care system is designed — not just what individual providers do, but how the system itself works — what would it be?

If I could change one thing, it would be the way the system handles the "expertise" of its patients. We have this pervasive culture in maternity care where, if a woman—especially a healthcare worker—shows she knows the terminology or understands the physiology, the staff immediately pivots to a "peer-to-peer" clinical exchange. They stop offering the soft, supportive, human care that is the bedrock of midwifery and nursing. The system assumes that because you *know* what’s happening, you don’t need someone to *hold space* for what you’re feeling. 

It’s a massive blind spot. We need to design a system that explicitly decouples a patient’s "clinical literacy" from their emotional needs. I want a system that checks in on the *person* first, regardless of their credentials. We shouldn’t have to choose between being respected as a professional and being supported as a mother. I’d love to see a mandatory, dedicated component of care—maybe a "psychosocial debrief"—that doesn't just ask about bleeding or baby weight, but asks, "How is the *you* that’s becoming a mother?" 

We treat the physical birth as a clinical event to be managed, but we leave the identity shift completely unaddressed. I want a system that acknowledges the vulnerability of that transition as much as it prioritizes the safety of the vitals.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

You know, there is one final thing that feels like it’s been sitting right under the surface of all these clinical conversations. It’s the grief. 

I don't think the system is built to handle the grief of a mother who feels like she lost her "old self" in the process of birth. When I talk about my transition, it sounds like I’m talking about a job change or a difficult clinical rotation. But the truth is, I’m mourning the woman I was before I walked through those hospital doors. I’m mourning the version of me who thought her knowledge was enough to protect her from the messiness of life. 

I think we have this implicit expectation that if you’re a "successful" mother—if you’re educated, if you’re healthy, if you’ve had a safe delivery—you should just be grateful and move on. There isn't a space in our current system for that quiet, persistent mourning. I want us to be able to acknowledge that you can be incredibly joyful about your child, and at the exact same time, be devastated by the loss of the woman you used to be. It shouldn't be a dirty little secret you keep in the breakroom. It should be a standard part of the human experience we check in on. Thank you for listening. It… it really did help to say that out loud.
